items.title: Cadre is best modeled as a B2B2C support platform with a strong member product core
items.summary: Public navigation and pricing show Cadre serves individuals directly, teams through employers, and contributors who supply content and support.
items.implication: Model members, employer buyers, program administrators, and contributors as distinct strategic customer groups rather than collapsing the business into a single buyer persona.

items.title: Immediate access and low-friction participation are central to the value proposition
items.summary: Cadre repeatedly emphasizes 24/7 access, daily live sessions, mobile and desktop availability, and fast access without traditional care delays.
items.implication: Treat support discovery, live participation, reminders, and habit loops as first-class product areas with measurable activation and repeat-use KPIs.

kpis: Time to first meaningful support interaction

items.title: Cadre's product promise depends on trusted contributor supply
items.summary: Public materials emphasize vetted contributors, daily livestreams, expert and peer-led content, and contributor onboarding.
items.implication: Model contributor sourcing, profile quality, session tooling, moderation, and payouts as core supply-side capabilities.

items.title: Employer value depends on activation and perceived usefulness, not just enrollment
items.summary: Cadre's employer positioning focuses on higher engagement than traditional EAP models, including a public testimonial that 98% of employees found the benefit useful.
items.implication: Employer-facing analytics and rollout workflows should measure activation, repeat engagement, live-event participation, and renewal confidence, not only seat counts.

items.title: Privacy, anonymity, and safe-community controls are differentiating trust requirements
items.summary: Cadre explicitly promotes privacy-friendly participation, anonymity guidance, confidential handling, and a safer alternative to unchecked public platforms.
items.implication: Identity, consent, moderation, escalation, and employer data separation should be treated as foundational capabilities rather than secondary compliance tasks.
